theme: What a CFO said 6 months after the trip — unscripted

linkedin.text:
"I was the no vote." — a CFO, six months after the trip he tried to kill.

We don't collect testimonials at the end of a trip. Everyone loves an event while they're in it. We ask CFOs 6 months later, when the answer actually means something.

Here's one, this March, about a trip we produced last October:

"€485k for 130 people during a hiring freeze. I asked the CMO what changes if we don't do this. She had no number — so I approved it on her instinct and my reluctance.

Six months in: the sales team that traveled closed 38% more in Q4 than Q3. Two R&D engineers who'd been quietly interviewing elsewhere withdrew — roughly €380k in replacement cost we didn't spend. The trip's WhatsApp group is still active; internal questions get answered in 11 minutes.

I was wrong. Not because I miscalculated — because I was measuring the wrong thing. The ROI isn't on the post-trip survey. It's in the operational metrics 90-180 days later. Next year I'm pre-approving the budget in Q1."

— CFO, Israeli industrial tech, 280 employees.

That 6-month reality is what we build for.

📅 Book a free 30-minute call and we'll show you where your ROI actually shows up.

"I was the no vote." Then he saw the 6-month numbers. 📊

We ask CFOs how they feel 6 months after a trip — not during it. Here's one, this March:

💬 "€485k for 130 people during a hiring freeze. I asked what changes if we don't do this. The CMO had no number.

Six months in:
→ Sales closed 38% more in Q4 vs Q3
→ 2 engineers withdrew outside offers (~€380k avoided)
→ Trip WhatsApp group still active — Qs answered in 11 min

I was wrong. I was measuring the wrong thing. The ROI isn't on the survey — it's in the operational metrics 90-180 days later. Next year I'm pre-approving in Q1."

facebook.text:
A CFO told us he was the "no vote" on a €485k trip during a hiring freeze. 📊

We don't ask how a trip went the week after. We ask six months later — when the honeymoon is over and the numbers are in.

Here's what he told us this March:

💬 "Sales were up 38% in Q4 vs Q3. Two senior engineers who'd been interviewing elsewhere withdrew — about €380k we didn't spend replacing them. The trip's group chat is still active.

I was wrong. I was measuring the wrong thing. The ROI shows up 90-180 days later."

That's exactly what we build for.

When do you measure the value of an offsite? Tell us in the comments. 💬
